dimensions.title: How You Connect
dimensions.tagline: The difference between feeling loved and just being in a relationship.
dimensions.description: Identifies how you naturally give and receive affection using vivid forced-choice pairs. The goal is not to match -- it's to ensure what you give is what they need.
dimensions.scientific_basis: Based on affection preference research, operationalized through forced-choice methodology similar to Egbert & Polk's (2006) Connection Styles Scale. Forced-choice format reduces social desirability bias.
dimensions.compatibility_type_rationale: What matters is whether Partner A's giving style matches Partner B's receiving style, and vice versa.

sub_scales.title: Primary Receiving Language
sub_scales.description: How you most prefer to receive love.

sub_scales.title: Primary Giving Language
sub_scales.description: How you naturally express love to your partner.

questions.text: Which one would make your whole week?

a.text: A surprise weekend getaway, just the two of you

b.text: They cook your favorite meal and run you a bath after a long day

questions.text: Which hits different?

a.text: They post you on their birthday with a paragraph about why you matter

b.text: They write you a private love letter you find on your pillow

questions.scoring_note: Both map to verbal_appreciation but distinguish public vs. private expression preference.

questions.text: After the worst week of your life, which heals you more?

a.text: They pull you close and just hold you. No words needed.

b.text: They handle everything -- groceries, laundry, dinner -- so you don't have to think

questions.text: Your partner just crushed a big goal. How do you celebrate them?

a.text: Plan a night out or experience they'll never forget

b.text: Buy them something meaningful that marks the moment

questions.text: Random Tuesday night. How do you show love without being asked?

a.text: Phone away, full attention, asking about their day like you mean it

b.text: Walk up behind them, wrap your arms around them, and just stay there

questions.text: Your partner seems down. What's your first instinct?

a.text: Tell them exactly what you love about them and why they matter

b.text: Take something off their plate without being asked -- cook, clean, handle it

scoring.compatibility_function: Check if Partner A's giving style matches Partner B's receiving style, and vice versa. Full match = 1.0, one-direction = 0.6, no match = 0.2.

dimensions.title: Hot Takes & Dealbreakers
dimensions.tagline: Pop culture debates that accidentally reveal your values.
dimensions.description: Uses cultural moments, relationship hot takes, and dealbreaker scenarios to surface values and boundaries that standard assessments miss. These questions feel fun but measure real constructs.
dimensions.scientific_basis: Values measurement through indirect assessment. Research by Schwartz (2012) and Rokeach (1973) shows that value priorities are more accurately captured through situational responses than direct self-report. Cultural scenario methodology adapted from Triandis' (1995) subjective culture approach.
dimensions.compatibility_type_rationale: Dealbreakers and hot takes reveal boundary alignment. Partners with similar boundary frameworks experience less friction.

sub_scales.title: Boundaries & Trust
sub_scales.description: Where you draw lines around privacy, trust, and personal space.

sub_scales.title: Gender & Power Dynamics
sub_scales.description: How you navigate traditional vs. progressive relationship roles.

sub_scales.title: Vulnerability & Depth
sub_scales.description: How quickly and deeply you open up about the big stuff.

questions.text: Prenup: smart move or trust issue?
questions.scoring_note: Low = trust-first orientation. High = pragmatic/protective orientation.

questions.text: Your partner still talks to their ex 'as friends.' Red flag or green flag?
questions.scoring_note: Low = strict boundaries. High = flexible/mature boundaries.

questions.text: Is it ever okay to go through your partner's phone?
questions.scoring_note: Low = strong privacy values. High = transparency/openness values. Neither is wrong; used for similarity matching.

questions.text: Should the man always pay on the first date?
questions.scoring_note: Low = traditional gender role expectations. High = egalitarian dynamics.

questions.text: Your partner makes significantly more money than you. Does that change the dynamic?
questions.scoring_note: Low = income affects power dynamic. High = income is decoupled from relationship dynamic.

questions.text: Kids or no kids -- is that a day-one conversation or a year-two conversation?
questions.scoring_note: Low = direct/efficient communicator about big topics. High = organic/patient communicator.

questions.text: Beyonce or Rihanna? And this says more about you than you think.
questions.scoring_note: Cultural alignment proxy. Low = discipline/legacy/excellence orientation. High = authenticity/freedom orientation.

questions.text: Your partner gains 30 pounds. How does that actually affect things for you?
questions.scoring_note: Measures honesty about physical attraction vs. unconditional acceptance. No right answer; similarity matching.

scoring.sub_scale_computation: Each sub-scale = mean of its items. Range: 1.0 - 5.0.
scoring.compatibility_function: similarity: pair_score = 1 - (|score_A - score_B| / 4). Per sub-scale similarity weighted equally.

dimensions.title: Emotional Intelligence
dimensions.tagline: Can you read a room? Can you read yourself?
dimensions.description: Measures your ability to recognize, understand, and manage emotions in yourself and in your relationships. High EQ predicts better conflict resolution, deeper emotional attunement, and more satisfying partnerships.
dimensions.scientific_basis: Adapted from the Wong & Law Emotional Intelligence Scale (WLEIS; Wong & Law, 2002). Items reframed as culturally resonant scenarios. Supplemented by Brackett, Warner, & Bosco (2005) linking EQ to relationship satisfaction.
dimensions.compatibility_type_rationale: Both partners benefit from similar EQ levels. Low-low pairings are high risk.

sub_scales.title: Self-Awareness
sub_scales.description: Recognizing your own emotions and understanding how they influence your behavior.

sub_scales.title: Empathy
sub_scales.description: Reading and responding to others' emotional states.

sub_scales.title: Emotional Regulation
sub_scales.description: Managing your emotional reactions under stress.

questions.text: Your friend is clearly going through it but says 'I'm fine.' You...
How often do you cry? And more importantly, do you feel weird about it?
Someone cuts you off in traffic. Scale of 1-10, how long does that ruin your mood?
Your partner shares something vulnerable and you don't relate to it at all. You...
You can usually tell when your partner is upset before they say a single word.
A coworker you don't really like is having a terrible day. What's your honest internal reaction?
You're upset about something your partner did. How long before you bring it up?
Your partner says something that triggers an old wound. You feel the anger rising. What happens next?
Bad day at work. Your partner did nothing wrong but they're right there. How good are you at not taking it out on them?
When I feel myself getting jealous, I can sit with that feeling instead of immediately acting on it.

scoring.compatibility_function: similarity_with_floor: if either scores below 2.5, apply 0.6 penalty. Raw similarity = 1 - (|A - B| / 4). Level bonus applied for high-high pairings.

paid_dimensions.title: Lifestyle & Ambition
paid_dimensions.tagline: How you spend your time reveals what you value.
paid_dimensions.description: Captures how you prefer to spend your time, energy, and attention. Mismatches here don't cause dramatic fights -- they cause slow erosion.
paid_dimensions.scientific_basis: Draws from Big Five personality subscales: Conscientiousness and Openness to Experience, adapted from IPIP-NEO-120.

scoring.compatibility_function: similarity: pair_score = 1 - (|A - B| / 4). Sub-scale similarities weighted.

paid_dimensions.title: Intimacy & Chemistry
paid_dimensions.tagline: The one that actually matters. Science-backed, no fluff.
paid_dimensions.description: Measures sexual compatibility across three evidence-based dimensions: how desire works for you (spontaneous vs. responsive), how openly you communicate about intimacy, and your day-to-day physical affection needs.
paid_dimensions.scientific_basis: Grounded in Basson's circular model of sexual response (2000) for desire dynamics, the New Sexual Satisfaction Scale (NSSS; Stulhofer et al., 2010) for intimacy communication, and attachment-informed physical affection research.

scoring.compatibility_function: similarity: pair_score = 1 - (|A - B| / 4). Intimacy communication weighted slightly higher as strongest predictor of sexual satisfaction.

metadata.notes: Dimensions 4-8 complete the CompatibleIQ assessment. 5 free dimensions (1-5) total weight = 75. 3 paid dimensions (6-8) total weight = 25. Combined = 100.
